mechanism: Triterpenes (for example asiaticoside and madecassoside derivatives) are associated with collagen-related signaling, microcirculatory effects, and anti-inflammatory pathways in preclinical work. Clinical mechanism confirmation remains incomplete.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. Folk medicine. Folk medicine
safetyNotes: Usually tolerated in short-term use, but headache, GI upset, and sedation can occur. Rare hepatotoxicity cases have been reported with some gotu kola products; discontinue and seek medical assessment if liver-injury symptoms develop.
description: Centella asiatica is used traditionally for skin healing and as a tonic. Modern studies most often examine triterpene-rich preparations for wound-related outcomes or chronic venous insufficiency symptoms; some positive findings exist, but many studies are small or use heterogeneous products.
dosage: No broadly accepted single dose across indications. Prefer standardized products used in clinical studies and avoid prolonged unsupervised high-dose use.

interactions: Potential additive sedation with CNS depressants is plausible; monitor for drowsiness.
Given rare liver-injury reports, use caution with hepatotoxic drugs and avoid multi-ingredient products of unclear quality.
preparation: Used as dried herb infusion, topical formulations, and standardized oral triterpene extracts. Evidence is formulation-specific.
region: Native to tropical and subtropical Asia, with distribution in parts of Africa and Oceania; cultivated widely for medicinal use.
summary: Centella asiatica (gotu kola) is used for wound and venous-support indications; evidence suggests possible benefit in selected preparations, but overall certainty remains limited.
